question: What tool brands are good for basic home repairs and small DIY projects?
answer_text:
For basic home repairs and small DIY projects, you don’t need the most expensive pro-grade tools. Here are reliable brands that balance quality and value:

Best Value / “Good Enough” for Most Homeowners

Ryobi – Huge ecosystem of affordable cordless tools (drill, driver, multi-tool). Perfect for occasional use.

Black+Decker – Great for very light tasks. Their screwdrivers, wrenches, and basic power tools are fine for assembling furniture or small fixes.

Hart (Walmart) – Surprisingly decent for the price; good entry-level cordless lineup.

Step-Up Quality (Still DIY-Friendly)

Craftsman – Reliable hand tools (sockets, wrenches, pliers) with an easy warranty. Their power tools are solid for regular home use.

Skil – Old brand revived with good budget power tools (especially their 20V series).

Worx – Mostly known for outdoor power tools, but their rotary tools and compact drills are handy for small projects.

Hand Tools Only (Brands Worth Buying)

Husky (Home Depot) – Excellent warranty on wrenches, ratchets, pliers. Easy to replace.

Kobalt (Lowe’s) – Comfortable grips and decent steel for screwdrivers, hammers, tape measures.

Stanley – Classic for basic hammers, levels, tape measures, and utility knives.

Avoid for Basic DIY (Overkill or Overpriced)

DeWalt, Milwaukee, Makita – Pro-grade, great but expensive. If you see a deal, buy it, but you don’t need them for hanging shelves or fixing a leaky faucet.

Snap-on, Matco – Completely unnecessary for home use.

My simple recommendation:

Cordless drill/driver – Ryobi or Craftsman.

Hand tool starter set (screwdrivers, pliers, wrench, hammer) – Husky or Stanley.

Measuring and leveling – Stanley or generic store brand.

One tip: For small DIY, a good #2 Phillips screwdriver (e.g., Klein or Wera, you can buy singletons) and a quality 6-in-1 or 11-in-1 screwdriver will make you happier than a full cheap set.
